aliases: Hong Kong milk tea with coffee

english: Hong Kong milk tea with coffee

region_of_origin: Hong Kong; widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Yuan Yang (coffee-tea blend). Mapped to broader beverage food product class. A more specific term for mixed coffee-tea beverages is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea-based beverages (broader category anchor for mixed tea-coffee drink)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Yuan Yang. A0FJP (tea-based beverages) is used as the closest defensible anchor. Coffee component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Yuan Yang or mixed coffee-tea beverage as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of Yuan Yang is approximately 250 ml (250 g), as typically served at a Singapore kopitiam or Hong Kong cha chaan teng.

nutrient_source.title: Yuan Yang (Coffee with Milk Tea) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yuan Yang (Coffee with Milk Tea), CR ID D09020079. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for sweetened milk tea / coffee-tea blend
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yuan Yang in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to sweetened milk tea with condensed milk (GI ~50–55 range per University of Sydney GI Database entries for tea with sugar and milk). The dominant glycaemic driver is the added sugar (sucrose from condensed milk, GI ~65) diluted in a predominantly water-based beverage. A blended GI of 55 is at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification system. gi_category is therefore 'low'. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 6.56 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 9.0 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar from condensed milk (5.4 g/100 g) qualifies Yuan Yang as a sugar-sweetened beverage; habitual SSB consumption independently raises blood pressure risk (AHA 2018) despite low sodium content (17 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request 'ko tong' (no sugar) and specify evaporated milk only to reduce SSB-related blood pressure risk; sodium content alone is low but the sugar load remains the primary cardiovascular concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Yuan Yang is a sugar-sweetened beverage with added sugar from condensed milk (5.4 g sugar/100 g as consumed); liquid sugar raises postprandial blood glucose rapidly and SSBs are independently associated with increased type 2 diabetes risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid standard Yuan Yang and request an unsweetened version ('ko tong') with evaporated milk only; even 'less sugar' variants remain SSBs and should be consumed only occasionally with careful glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from condensed milk (estimated ~1.0 g/100 g) and added sugar (5.4 g/100 g) contribute to atherogenic risk; habitual SSB intake is associated with elevated triglycerides and adverse lipid profiles.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Yuan Yang to occasional consumption and request evaporated milk with no added sugar to reduce saturated fat and sugar-driven triglyceride elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar and saturated fat from condensed milk promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity, negating the polyphenol content of the tea and coffee components.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened version ('ko tong') with evaporated milk to reduce sugar-driven and saturated-fat-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yuan Yang (鸳鸯, also romanized as Yuen Yeung) is a popular Hong Kong-origin beverage widely consumed in Singapore kopitiams and Hong Kong cha chaan tengs. It is a blend of approximately one-third brewed coffee and two-thirds Hong Kong-style milk tea (strong black tea with evaporated or condensed milk), sweetened with condensed milk and sometimes additional sugar. The name 鸳鸯 (Mandarin: yuān yāng; Cantonese: yuen yeung) refers to the mandarin duck, symbolising the harmonious pairing of two contrasting elements — coffee and tea. The drink delivers a complex flavour profile: the bitterness and roasted depth of coffee balanced by the astringency of strong black tea and the creamy sweetness of condensed milk. Per 100 g, Yuan Yang provides 44 kcal, 0.82 g protein, 1.59 g fat, and 6.56 g carbohydrate (5.39 g sugar), with low sodium (17 mg) and modest dietary fibre (0.51 g). As a sugar-sweetened beverage, it is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension without modification. Customisation options include 'ko tong' (no sugar) and specifying evaporated milk instead of condensed milk to reduce glycaemic and saturated fat load. Yuan Yang is most commonly consumed at breakfast or as an afternoon drink and is a defining item of Singapore and Hong Kong hawker and café culture.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Yuan Yang?
Is Yuan Yang suitable for hypertension?
What is the difference between Yuan Yang and milk tea?
How much sugar is in Yuan Yang?
Can I drink Yuan Yang if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D09020079) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea, clearly documented.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 1.04 g/100 g based on typical condensed milk and evaporated milk fat composition; HPB source reports total fat (1.59 g/100 g) but does not separately itemise saturated fat — this value is an internal estimate consistent with the dairy fat fraction of the beverage. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for Yuan Yang as a prepared beverage is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. The input dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' are retained as contextual notes only; these tags are not clinically endorsed for individuals with diabetes or hypertension given the SSB classification. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for Yuan Yang has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Per the University of Sydney GI classification system, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'; GI 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category and gi_category has been corrected to 'low' accordingly.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values (iteration 2).
Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values (iteration 2).

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low': GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI classification system. Previous iteration incorrectly applied the medium threshold (55–69). gi_source notes updated to reflect this classification rationale (iteration 3).
